narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 missense change c.3024G>A (p.Met1008Ile) occurs in exon 11 of the gene and has been reported in breast and ovarian cancer patients since the mid‑1990s [8776600].

Clinical investigations in diverse populations have repeatedly identified carriers of this variant but have not demonstrated a clear disease association. In a cohort of high‑risk Ashkenazi Jewish women two individuals carried M1008I without evidence of increased risk, and the variant was not enriched among cases [18798010]. Subsequent studies in Libyan, Algerian, Tunisian, Bahraini, Chilean and Mexican cohorts likewise reported the allele in breast/ovarian cancer patients and classified it as benign or likely benign in clinical databases such as ClinVar (e.g., rs1800704) [PMC11210411][PMC2452980][PMC7962399][PMC6625152][PMC5650336][PMC6327376].

Functional assays provide further support for a non‑pathogenic role. A homology‑directed repair assay showed that M1008I retains ~99 % of wild‑type BRCA1 homologous recombination activity, indistinguishable from the wild‑type protein [PMC5432210]. Splice‑site testing found no aberrant splicing associated with the c.3024G>A change, indicating that the variant does not affect RNA processing [PMC4907813].

Population data reveal a low but detectable allele frequency across multiple ancestries (e.g., 0.02 % in gnomAD, 0.19 % in the Mexican cohort) and the variant is absent from disease‑specific enrichment analyses [PMC3984285][PMC6327376]. In silico predictions consistently label the substitution as benign or tolerated: PolyPhen‑2 and SIFT assign benign scores, REVEL and BayesDel classify it as benign, and Align‑GVGD places it in the lowest risk class (C0) [PMC8160182][PMC6726608][PMC7200594].

Collectively, the weight of clinical observations, functional experiments, population frequencies and computational predictions indicates that BRCA1 p.Met1008Ile is a benign polymorphism rather than a pathogenic mutation. No study in the extracted evidence set provides credible evidence of disease causality, and the variant is therefore regarded as non‑pathogenic in the current literature.

mentions.quoted_text: BACKGROUND: Three mutations in BRCA1 (185delAG 5382InsC) and BRCA2 (6174delT) can be detected in a substantial proportion of Jewish Ashkenazi breast/ovarian cancer families. Family-specific pathogenic mutations in both genes can be detected in up to 5% of high risk Ashkenazim. The contribution of major gene rearrangements and seemingly pathogenic missense mutations to inherited breast cancer predisposition has never been systematically evaluated in Ashkenazim. MATERIAL AND METHODS: High risk, Jewish Ashkenazi women, non-carriers of the predominant Jewish BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, were genotyped for major gene rearrangements in BRCA1/BRCA2 using Multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA), and for the occurrence rate of 6 seemingly pathogenic missense mutations in BRCA1 (R866C, R331S, R841W, Y179C, C61G, **M1008I**) using a modified restriction enzyme assay. RESULTS: Overall, 105 Jewish Ashkenazi high risk women, participated in the study: 104 with breast cancer [age at diagnosis (mean +/- SD) 51.05 +/- 11.13 years], one was affected with ovarian cancer (61 years). Two were found to carry the **M1008I** mutation in BRCA1 and none harbored any of the other missense mutations. MLPA reveled four changes (amplifications of exons 5, 17, 19 and 21) in BRCA1 in five patients, and six patients exhibited 4 MLPA-detectable abnormalities in BRCA2 (amplifications in exons 1b, 2, and deletions in exons 11a and 25). None of these abnormalities could be confirmed using quantitative PCR (qPCR) analysis. CONCLUSIONS: Major gene rearrangements involving BRCA1 BRCA2 contribute little to the burden of inherited predisposition of breast cancer in Ashkenazi Jews.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened high‑risk Ashkenazi Jewish women without common founder BRCA1/2 mutations and identified two carriers of the BRCA1 M1008I missense variant, while finding that major gene rearrangements contribute little to breast cancer predisposition in this population.
article_mentions.title: Analysis of BRCA1/BRCA2 genes' contribution to breast cancer susceptibility in high risk Jewish Ashkenazi women.

mentions.quoted_text: Inherited mutations in the BRCA1 gene are known to confer a predisposition to breast and ovarian cancer. We have first characterized 19 sequence variants in the BRCA1 gene during mutation screening by direct sequencing using DNA samples from breast/ovarian cancer patients or obligate carriers. The frequencies of these sequence variants were then compared with those found in control populations of women. Among the 10 sequence variants showing an estimated frequency of the less common allele above 0.05, Q/R356, L/P871, E/G1038, K/R1183 and S/G1613 result in a change of amino acids, 2201C/T, 2430T/C and 4427C/T are silent mutations and the two others, 4209-141C/A and 5272 + 66A/G, are intronic polymorphisms. These frequent polymorphisms, with the exception of Q/R356, were in complete or significant pairwise linkage disequilibrium as evaluated in our control populations. With one exception (L/P871), none of these variants had statistically significant (P < 0.05) differences in allele frequency between breast/ovarian cancer patients or obligate carriers and our control populations. Four rare sequence variants designated 710C-->T, D693N, R841W and S1040N were found in both unaffected and breast/ovarian cancer populations, while the missense mutations **M1008I**, E1219D, R1347G, T1561I and M1628V were detected only once in our patient population. When a functional test is available, it will be important to determine the consequence on the BRCA1 activity of these rare sequence variants and missense mutations.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identified the BRCA1 missense variant M1008I in a single breast/ovarian cancer patient without providing functional or pathogenicity interpretation.
article_mentions.title: Comparison of BRCA1 polymorphisms, rare sequence variants and/or missense mutations in unaffected and breast/ovarian cancer populations.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened BRCA1 in breast‑cancer patients and reported the Met1008Ile variant as computationally neutral (benign).',
article_mentions.title: Unraveling the Role of BRCA1 variants in Dysregulation of Transcriptional and Post-Transcriptional Mechanisms in Breast Cancer

mentions.quoted_text: BRCA1/2 mutations are rarely found in SCLC, including in the SCLC lines used in this study SCLC tested were wild type for BRCA1/2, while potential germline alterations were found in 2: H378 (**M1008I**) and H446 (D1496H)]. The mechanism of selective vulnerability of some SCLCs to PARPi is not understood. Talazoparib is a highly potent PARPi that blocked the formation of PARylation in NCI-H2081 (H2081) cells (a representative SCLC cell line) (Fig. 1A and fig. S1A). Besides its catalytic inhibition activity, talazoparib treatment also led to profound PARP1/2 trapping, gammaH2AX formation (a marker of DNA double-strand breaks), and PARP1 cleavage (a marker of apoptotic cell death) (fig. S1, B and C).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study demonstrates that PARP inhibitor sensitivity in small cell lung cancer is driven by degradation of lineage‑specific oncoproteins and notes that BRCA1 M1008I appears as a rare potential germline alteration in one cell line, without further functional interpretation.
article_mentions.title: Induced degradation of lineage-specific oncoproteins drives the therapeutic vulnerability of small cell lung cancer to PARP inhibitors

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The Mexican population study reports that the BRCA1 M1008I (c.3024G>A) variant is classified as benign in clinical databases while describing overall BRCA variant frequencies in the cohort.
article_mentions.title: Population and breast cancer patients' analysis reveals the diversity of genomic variation of the BRCA genes in the Mexican population

article_mentions.title: Pathogenic variants in human DNA damage repair genes mostly arose in recent human history

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The paper describes DriverDetect, a machine learning framework that combines several existing prediction tools to more accurately identify deleterious cancer mutations, and includes computational scores for the BRCA1 M1008I variant.
article_mentions.title: Machine learning optimized DriverDetect software for high precision prediction of deleterious mutations in human cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents spliceogenicity testing for BRCA1 p.M1008I and finds no splice aberration, indicating the variant likely does not affect splicing and is unlikely pathogenic via this mechanism.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents splicing‑focused ACMG/AMP guidance and includes a supplementary table that records BRCA1 M1008I (c.3024G>A) with a SpliceAI score of 0 and no observed splicing effect, but does not provide an explicit pathogenicity interpretation for this variant.
article_mentions.title: APPLICATION OF THE ACMG/AMP FRAMEWORK TO CAPTURE EVIDENCE RELEVANT TO PREDICTED AND OBSERVED IMPACT ON SPLICING: RECOMMENDATIONS FROM THE CLINGEN SVI SPLICING SUBGROUP

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study evaluates classification methods for BRCA1/2 unclassified variants and reports that M1008I is classified as benign/neutral in the supplemental data, while overall the methods help identify high‑risk BRCA1 UV carriers based on family‑history correlations.

article_mentions.title: Germline Variation in Cancer-Susceptibility Genes in a Healthy, Ancestrally Diverse Cohort: Implications for Individual Genome Sequencing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study finds that current in‑silico tools have low specificity and are generally unsuitable for pathogenicity classification of BRCA1/2 missense variants, although SIFT and MutationTaster2 may reliably predict benign variants.
article_mentions.title: Performance of in silico prediction tools for the classification of rare BRCA1/2 missense variants in clinical diagnostics

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies the BRCA1 p.M1008I missense variant in ovarian cancer samples and includes it in a loss‑of‑heterozygosity analysis without assigning a pathogenicity classification.
article_mentions.title: Patterns and functional implications of rare germline variants across 12 cancer types

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study finds that individual mutation effect prediction algorithms vary widely in accuracy and that combining multiple predictors modestly improves overall performance, especially negative predictive value, but no single tool is sufficient for reliable clinical or experimental decision‑making.
article_mentions.title: Benchmarking mutation effect prediction algorithms using functionally validated cancer-related missense mutations

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article introduces the GESPA tool and, in its supplementary data, classifies the BRCA1 M1008I variant as a polymorphism, suggesting it is likely benign.
article_mentions.title: GESPA: classifying nsSNPs to predict disease association

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents a phylogenetic analysis of cancer driver genes and includes p.M1008I among the surveyed variants, but provides no functional, clinical, or pathogenicity interpretation for this specific variant.
article_mentions.title: Deep phylogeny of cancer drivers and compensatory mutations
